behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is local backoff math/state progression, with no required module handoff, I/O, or model output. Higher levels were considered and rejected because the behavior text is purely about the doubling rule and cap, which can be reproduced with fixed inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Test a single backoff function/object with attempt counts and max cap config; assert sequence (e.g., 100, 200, 400...) and clamping at configured maximum.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This failure typically appears at the boundary between response classification and retry/backoff policy (multiple modules wired together). System was considered but rejected: behavior only says identify a rate-limit response and compute wait, which is reproducible with in-process stubbed responses (no real network required).
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry policy + response parser/classifier; feed synthetic 429/limit-exceeded responses (including headers/body variants) and assert retry decision plus computed delay.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Requires orchestration across timer/scheduler + request executor + response handling modules. System was considered and rejected because the behavior can be reproduced with fake timers and a stub transport; no real OS/network semantics are required by the text.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake clock and stub transport: first call returns rate-limit error, next call succeeds. Assert original request payload is retried, second attempt occurs after expected delay, and caller receives success without extra retry-handling code.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Needs retry-counter termination plus error translation at module boundaries (retry engine -> public API error surface). Unit was plausible, but the behavior text includes 'after configured maximum number of retries', which ties correctness to orchestration state, not just a pure mapper.
behaviors.test_description: Configure low max retries; stub repeated rate-limit failures; assert final rejection is `QuotaExhaustedError` type/class and not generic transport/error wrapper.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: This is explicitly cross-module ('initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking') and must validate termination invariants over the composed flow. System was considered and rejected because indefinite-loop/termination can be caught with deterministic stubs + fake clock; no real network/subprocess requirement appears in the behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Run composed client with fake clock and controlled failing responses; assert bounded number of attempts, monotonic time advancement per backoff, and eventual surfaced terminal error within expected virtual time/attempt limits.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is shared state handoff under concurrency across caller-facing API and quota manager modules. System was considered and rejected using behavior evidence: 'in the same process' indicates in-process async coordination is the target, reproducible without real external infra.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn multiple concurrent `call()` invocations against one client instance with stubbed transport; assert a single shared quota counter/bucket is consumed globally (not per-caller), including contention scenarios.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Ordering correctness emerges from queueing + dispatcher + retry/quota gating interaction, not a single isolated function in most implementations. System was considered and rejected because FIFO dispatch can be validated with in-process scheduler control and stub transport; no real OS/network behavior is required by the text.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue multiple calls rapidly while quota gate blocks sends; release capacity and assert dispatch order matches submission order under concurrent arrivals and retries.
